name: Philosophy Applied Ethics Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: There is a genuinely coherent applied-ethics backbone: PHIL 333 Moral Philosophy, PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics, PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics, PHIL 310 Logic, and PHIL 355 The Philosophy of Science form a strong, well-matched cluster for a technical university. Coherence is limited chiefly by dilution - many COM/PSY/LIT electives sit in the same menu - and by an empty requirementGroups with no declared required core.
dimensions.suggestion: Declare a required ethics core (Moral Philosophy + Logic + one applied ethics) and mark cross-disciplinary courses as supporting electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. Most PHIL courses sit behind the ENGL 102 + 200-level GER gateway (depth 4); the deepest chain (6) comes from the broader pool, not from philosophy-internal stacking, which is appropriate for a minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Ensure at least one gateway ethics course is reachable directly after ENGL 102 so students can enter the minor early.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, so the required credit count is not published, and creditReconcileDelta is null (an expected data caveat for a menu-style minor with no term plan). Courses are uniformly 3 credits, so distribution within the menu is even, but the total is opaque.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the total credit requirement and the split between a required ethics core and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive, but the program page carries no description, no requirementGroups, and no stated credits, so the strong ethics core is not visible as a structured pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description and group the strong PHIL ethics core visibly ahead of the cross-listed electives.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Applied ethics is highly current and this menu is well-aimed for NJIT: PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics and PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics speak directly to engineering and health students, and PHIL 355 Philosophy of Science supports research integrity. The clearest gap is a dedicated AI/data-ethics course as the field's fastest-growing area.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an AI and data ethics course connecting PHIL 334 with STS 375 AI and the Human Mind and STS 350 Computers and Society.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled PHIL/PSY/LIT descriptions are present and substantive; none are flagged thin or missing (PSY 361 carries an abbreviated title, 'Found of Cyberpsychology II').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references; internal chains such as PSY 361 -> PSY 359 are sensible. One inconsistency: PHIL 331 lists 'HUM 102' where sibling courses list 'ENGL 102', and most courses cite a GER category rather than a specific course.

missingTopics.rationale: There is no dedicated course on algorithmic bias, privacy, and autonomous systems; PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics is adjacent but general.
STS 360 Ethics and the Environment exists in the catalog but is not surfaced in the visible ethics core; it should be cross-listed.
Organizational and corporate ethics are absent; HRM 310 is management practice, not normative ethics.
PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics is a start, but CRISPR, neurotech, and genome-editing questions are not covered.
Rights, justice, and distributive theory - the backbone of policy ethics - are not represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: The fastest-growing applied-ethics area; bridge PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics with STS 375 AI and the Human Mind.
Connect to PSY 359 Foundations of Cyberpsychology and STS 350 Computers and Society for a technically grounded treatment.
Extend PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics into gene editing, neurotech, and human enhancement.
Link the ethics core to STS 360 Ethics and the Environment and STS 363 Sustainability Studies.

emergingTrends.trend: Ethics of automation & the future of work
emergingTrends.rationale: A professional-responsibility angle increasingly central to engineering and business practice.

topSuggestions: Add a dedicated AI/data-ethics course bridging PHIL 334, STS 375, and STS 350.
Cross-list environmental ethics (STS 360) and add business/professional ethics.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and define a required ethics core vs. electives.
Modernize PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics to include neuroethics and genome-editing.
